writing-text-into-files.label: Writing Text into Files
writing-text-into-files.summary: Use echo, >, >>, and cat to put real text into files.

sketch1.title: Use `>` to write or replace a file

sc-echo-purpose.title: What `echo` does
sc-echo-purpose.hint: Choose `>` for the first complete line and `>>` only when existing content must remain.

help.concept: `echo` produces text, `>` writes or replaces a file, and `>>` appends without removing earlier lines.
help.hint_1: Choose `>` for the first complete line and `>>` only when existing content must remain.
help.hint_2: Read the file with `cat` and check the exact line order before submitting. The current task asks: What does `echo "Hello"` do by itself?

echo-prints-text.title: `echo` prints text
echo-prints-text.bodyMarkdown:
`echo` sends text to terminal output. It is useful for checking wording before saving it.

```bash
echo "Workshop opens at 9"
```

The sentence appears in the terminal, but no file changes. Quotation marks keep the words together and are especially helpful when the text contains spaces. A common mistake is assuming visible output was saved automatically. In the next sketches, redirection symbols connect that output to a file.

write-with-greater-than.title: Use `>` to write or replace a file
write-with-greater-than.bodyMarkdown:
The `>` redirection symbol sends command output into a file. It creates the file when needed and **replaces all existing content** when the file already exists.

```bash
echo "Packing checklist" > packing.txt
cat packing.txt
```

The first command writes one line; the second verifies it. Because `>` overwrites, inspect an important file before using it and make a backup when the original must be preserved. Use `>` for the first line of a new file or when replacement is the explicit goal.

append-with-double-greater-than.title: Use `>>` to add another line
append-with-double-greater-than.bodyMarkdown:
The `>>` symbol appends new output to the end of a file instead of replacing what is already there.

```bash
echo "Packing checklist" > packing.txt
echo "Passport" >> packing.txt
cat packing.txt
```

The final file contains both lines in order. Use `>` once to establish the file, then `>>` for additions. Reversing the symbols can erase earlier work or create duplicate lines. Always read the finished file before moving on.
